title: NeoHospital: IAM evidence request — SSO assertions, SCIM deltas, RBAC samples
preview:
From: Jamie Ortiz <jamie.ortiz@neohospital.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Rivera <alex.rivera@neohospital.com>
Date: 2026-11-10T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Request: SSO / SCIM / RBAC samples for SOC 2 evidence

Hi Priya,

Thanks again for the webinar last week. NeoHospital is kicking off a scoped SOC 2 readiness review and our auditor has asked for a narrow set of artifacts related to SSO and provisioning for the applications Redwood federates with. Specifically they'd like: 

- 5 representative SAML assertion samples across two SPs (including the NameID and attributes, timestamps)
- SCIM delta logs (last 30 days) for user provisioning and deprovisioning events
- RBAC ownership mapping: role -> permission list + owner for three primary app integrations
- Session activity samples showing session creation and expiry for web SSO (timezone: PST)

Can you confirm whether Redwood can provide these artifacts redacted for PII, and a short delivery plan (timeline + contact for the SFTP/secure transfer)? We are on a tight window — auditor wants the package by 2026-11-18.

Appreciate it,

Jamie Ortiz
Security Manager, NeoHospital


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Jamie Ortiz <jamie.ortiz@neohospital.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-10T11:04:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: SSO / SCIM / RBAC samples for SOC 2 evidence

Hi Jamie — thanks for the clear list. We can provide everything you asked for and will redact PII before transfer. Proposed workback: 

1) We’ll collect the 5 SAML assertions (earliest-to-latest samples) and remove any user-identifying fields, leaving NameID type/timestamp/attributes schema.
2) Export SCIM delta records for the last 30 days and provide a short CSV summary plus raw deltas in CSV.
3) RBAC mapping: export role -> permissions for the three apps you named during the call (AlertsApp, ChartSync, BillingUI) and include owner email and last modified.
4) Session activity: 48 sample rows showing session_start, session_end, ttl, and auth_method.

Delivery: We can stage the package in a secure SFTP location by 2026-11-17 EOD PT. Ben will be the lead for redaction and Mina will own the SCIM export.

I’ll attach a short workback (IAM_Evidence_Workback.xlsx) with milestones. If SFTP is not acceptable, we can use an expiring SharePoint link.

Action items for NeoHospital: confirm acceptable redaction pattern (hashing vs pseudonymization) and whether the auditor needs assertion raw XML or JSON translation.

Thanks — Priya
Priya Natarajan
Enterprise Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Cc: Jamie Ortiz <jamie.ortiz@neohospital.com>
Date: 2026-11-11T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Internal: NeoHospital IAM evidence — scope and extraction notes

Ben / Mina / Jonas — forwarding NeoHospital's ask (below). Quick notes for extraction: 

- SAML assertions: pull five assertions from the auth-log bucket (s3://auth-logs/prod/saml/2026/). Prefer recent window 2026-10-25 -> 2026-11-09. Keep one sample per SP and include one failed assertion example. Redact user.email and user.full_name; keep NameID type, AssertionIssueInstant, Audience, and attributes list.
- SCIM deltas: use scim-deltas-prod, export events where op in [\"create\",\"replace\",\"delete\"] for last 30 days. Provide CSV with columns: event_id, op, resourceType, id, timestamp, raw_payload (redacted). Max file size 10MB; if larger, deliver in split parts.
- RBAC mapping: run rbac-export job for Apps: AlertsApp, ChartSync, BillingUI. Include role_name, permission_list (comma-separated), owner_email, last_modified.
- Session activity: sample 48 lines from session-activity logs (filter by auth_method in [\"saml\",\"oauth2\"], timezone PST). Include ttl and device_type.

Redaction guidance: hash(email) using SHA256 and remove raw IPs; retain first 3 chars of user agent string. Auditor accepts hashed emails but asked for salt description (we will provide algorithm and note that salt is not included).

I'll coordinate packaging. Ben — can you confirm you can complete redaction and upload to sftp://uploads.redwood-inference.com/neohospital by Tue 11/17? Mina — confirm SCIM export size/format. Jonas — add a short README describing extraction steps and where in infra each artifact came from.

Thanks — Priya


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jamie Ortiz <jamie.ortiz@neohospital.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17T08:16:00-08:00
Subject: NeoHospital evidence package uploaded to SFTP (NEO-2304)

Jamie / Priya,

We uploaded the package to the secure sftp location you provided: sftp://uploads.redwood-inference.com/neohospital/NEO-2304-20261117/

Files included (redacted per request):
- saml_assertions_samples.jsonl (5 assertion objects: fields kept = AssertionIssueInstant, Audience, NameIDFormat, attributes schema; user emails hashed)
- scim_delta_20261017-20261109.csv (SCIM op deltas for last 30 days, raw_payload column redacted of PII)
- rbac_owners_mapping.csv (role_name, permission_list, owner_email_hashed, last_modified)
- session_activity_sample.csv (48 rows: session_start, session_end, ttl_seconds, auth_method, device_type)
- README_Neohospital_evidence.txt (extraction notes, hash algorithm details, locations in infra)

Notes on redaction: we hashed emails with SHA256 and removed raw IPs. The README contains the salt algorithm description and our redaction checklist. We included one intentionally failed SAML assertion to show error handling and audience mismatch trace.

If you want the raw XML assertions instead of JSONL, let us know and we'll prepare an encrypted, access-controlled transfer (requires NeoHospital written approval).

Ben Carter
Security & Compliance Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: saml_assertions_samples.jsonl (stub), scim_delta_20261017-20261109.csv (stub)

Date: 2026-11-17T15:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: NeoHospital evidence package uploaded to SFTP (NEO-2304)

Our auditor will pull these later today; we may follow up with a few clarifying questions around one assertion timestamp and the exact salt layout (won't require raw PII).

I’ll confirm once auditor completes initial review. Appreciate the responsiveness.

—Jamie

- Session activity: sample 48 lines from session-activity logs (filter by auth_method in [\"saml\",\"oauth2\"], timezone PST). Include ttl and device_type.

Redaction guidance: hash(email) using SHA256 and remove raw IPs; retain first 3 chars of user agent string. Auditor accepts hashed emails but asked for salt description (we will provide algorithm and note that salt is not included).

I'll coordinate packaging. Ben — can you confirm you can complete redaction and upload to sftp://uploads.redwood-inference.com/neohospital by Tue 11/17? Mina — confirm SCIM export size/format. Jonas — add a short README describing extraction steps and where in infra each artifact came from.

title: Deployment mode orientation + dedicated sizing clarifications
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samira Nadeem <samira@nimble-retail.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Nov 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: Deployment mode orientation + dedicated sizing clarifications

Hi Samira — thanks again for taking time earlier. Quick recap from our side plus the precise follow-up items we discussed so we can size a Dedicated pool if that’s the route you choose.

Key takeaways from today's call:
- Use case: real-time product Q&A and personalized recommendations in the storefront and mobile app.
- Traffic shape: predictable baseline with short daily spikes (marketing releases).
- Compliance: SOC 2 required; data residency in US.
- Desired durability: want access logs and token-level cost breakdowns for billing.

Decision point: Hosted API vs Dedicated vs Private VPC
- Hosted API: fastest to launch but less isolation and fewer custom GPU choices. Good for proof-of-concept.
- Dedicated: reserved GPU pool with guaranteed throughput and predictable latency — best fit once you need steady production volume and an SLA.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): for customers requiring full network isolation or air-gapped environments.

Things we need from Nimble to size Dedicated: (please reply inline)
1) Target steady-state TPS and expected 95th percentile TPS for bursts.
2) Expected concurrent requests and desired p99 latency (ms).
3) Token profile: average prompt tokens, average completion tokens, peak prompt length, whether context windows exceed 8k/32k.
4) Model family preference (if any) and appetite for quantized variants.
5) Region / VPC constraints and whether you’ll need cross-region failover.
6) Any procurement timing or contract constraints for committing to reserved capacity.

Attachments: I’ve attached the Redwood Dedicated options deck and a small token-profile sample you can edit.

Next steps: If you can send the TPS & token numbers this week, Karthik and Priyom will pull together a recommended GPU mix (H100 vs A100 80GB) and a 30/60/90 day plan (burst buffer, autoscale policy) — then we can schedule a 30-min sizing sync.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
(415) 555-0199

Attachment: redwood-dedicated-options.pdf (slide highlights)
Attachment: nimble_token_profile.csv (sample token distributions)


From: Samira Nadeem <samira@nimble-retail.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Mark Liu <mark.liu@nimble-retail.com>, devops@nimble-retail.com
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:28:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment mode orientation + dedicated sizing clarifications

Ben — appreciate the recap and clear checklist. Answers below (we copied engineering):

1) TPS / burst: steady-state target is ~12 TPS (product detail + quick answers across web + app), expected marketing spikes to ~55–65 TPS (95th percentile). We anticipate spikes lasting 10–20 minutes.
2) Concurrency / latency: typical concurrent in-flight requests ~8–12; during spikes up to ~50 concurrent. We want p99 generation latency under 400ms for short prompts (<= 512 tokens).
3) Token profile: average prompt tokens 60, average completion 320, 95th completion ~1,100 tokens (longer pass-through for multi-turn context). Peak context needs could touch 32k for some flows but only for <1% of requests.
4) Models / quantization: prefer open Llama family variants; we are open to quantized 4-bit variants if latency remains acceptable. Would like ability to fall back to fp16 on critical flows.
5) Region / VPC: US-East primary. We don't require a private VPC immediately but would prefer Dedicated with VPC peering later for stricter isolation.
6) Procurement: procurement is flexible; 6–12 month reserved commitment likely once sizing is validated.

Uploading our token profile and a short spec doc — see attached token csv. Happy to jump on the sizing sync after you propose a draft.

— Samira
Samira Nadeem
Head of ML Platform | Nimble Retail
samira@nimble-retail.com

Attachment: nimble_token_profile.csv (token distribution + example dialogues)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 11:07:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Deployment mode orientation + dedicated sizing clarifications

Forwarding Samira's reply — good detail. Quick internal notes and asks so we can get a sizing recommendation into the customer: 

Highlights to size around:
- Steady 12 TPS, spikes to 60 TPS; concurrent baseline ~10, spikes ~50. Peak tokens (1% of requests) up to 32k.
- Average completion ~320 tokens, 95th ~1100.
- Preference: Llama-family; open to 4-bit quant if we can guarantee p99 < 400ms for <=512 token responses.
- Region: us-east; VPC not immediately required but may be requested in rollout.

Specific asks for Priyom / Karthik:
1) Given the workload, initial dedicated pool proposal (GPU type(s), node count, per-node concurrency assumption). Provide two tiers: conservative (fp16 H100 mix) and cost-optimized (A100 80GB + quantized variants).
2) Sizing for a burst buffer: what headroom for 2x expected spike (i.e., ~120 TPS) on short notice? Estimate autoscale time and any cold-start impact on p99.
3) Whether we can satisfy 32k context for the small subset of requests: options and cost delta (model shards, memory-mapped layers, or offload).
4) Any config knobs (batching windows, per-model concurrency caps, KV cache TTL) we should propose to the customer to reduce tail latency while preserving throughput.

I'm going to propose a 30-min call this week for a quick sizing review — Priyom, can you draft numbers and we’ll present to Samira on Friday?

Ben


From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 17 Nov 2026 10:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Fwd: Deployment mode orientation + dedicated sizing clarifications — initial sizing

Ben / team — quick draft sizing based on Samira's numbers. Putting two initial options so we can discuss trade-offs on Friday:

Option A (performance-focused):
- GPU mix: 4 x H100 80GB (fp16) nodes in us-east as primary pool.
- Assumptions: per-GPU sustained throughput ~20–25 TPS for 512-token completions (Llama-like fp16). That gives steady headroom for 12 TPS with p99 < 350ms.
- Burst handling: autoscale to +2 nodes for short bursts (targeting handling up to ~80–100 TPS); recommend reserved burst buffer of 1 additional H100 for instant capacity.
- 32k context: require model sharding + offload; expect ~20–30% cost uplift compared with baseline.

Option B (cost-optimized):
- GPU mix: 6 x A100 80GB nodes with quantized 4-bit variants for non-critical flows, and a small fp16 pool (1x H100) for critical low-latency endpoints.
- Assumptions: 4-bit quant yields ~1.8–2x throughput for same GPU, but p99 may be ~10–30% higher. Good fit if Nimble is tolerant of slightly higher p99 on long completions.
- Burst handling: autoscale plan to add 3 A100s within 5–7 minutes; recommend short-lived burst policy for marketing spikes with graceful degradation to lower-fidelity quantized models if capacity is limited.

Operational / config notes:
- Batching windows: recommend dynamic batching with 30–50ms max latency window for non-critical calls.
- KV cache: set TTL 5 minutes for repeated product queries (reduces tokens/cost).
- Rollout: start on Hosted for 2–4 weeks to validate prompt mixes, then flip to Dedicated reserved pool with a 30-day canary (traffic split).

Next steps:
- I’ll populate the capacity-workbook.xlsx (Drive link) with node-level TPS numbers and cost deltas by EOD Wednesday.
- Ben: propose two 30-min slots on Friday for an internal review and then a customer sync.

Priyom Das
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
priyom.das@redwood.com

Related doc: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcExampleCapacitySheet (capacity-workbook.xlsx)

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: [INTERNAL] initial findings: silverline3b 5xx spike (KV cache eviction)

Marcus / Monica — quick sync notes from the triage:
- Symptom: sudden increase in 5xxs originating in the model-serving process when handling requests with context >8k tokens. Error path shows failed async disk spills from the KV cache layer followed by an unhandled exception in the request worker.
- Root cause (interim): an eviction storm on per-model KV shards caused by a corner-case in the LRU eviction where async spill tasks were delayed under IO pressure; when the spill failed the worker would surface a 5xx instead of gracefully falling back to in-memory degrade path.
- Evidence: attached kv-eviction-dump (sample of shard stats) and server traces correlate the spike with multiple parallel batch windows starting at ~08:28 PST.

Mitigations applied (10:00 PST):
1) Hotfix deployed to worker process to catch spill failures and route to the in-memory fallback (kv-spill-safety v1).
2) Increased KV in-memory threshold for silverline3b to reduce spill frequency for the next 48h.
3) Backpressure rule added: concurrent long-context batches for the route limited to 6 at once.

Next steps:
- Full patch to adjust eviction policy and improve async retry semantics (Marcus to own).
- Canary the fix into silverline3b-fallback tomorrow morning (small %).
- Postmortem to include monitoring hooks for pending async spill queue length and failed-spill-rate alarm.

I'll add timing notes to INF-4521 and attach the debug artifacts.

Rishi Malhotra
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference

Attachments: kv-eviction-dump-20261116.json.zip


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>

Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 16:42:00 -0800
Subject: silverline3b update: mitigation steps completed and next steps

Hi Priya / Ethan —

Quick update now that our mitigations have been applied and validated across your routes:

What happened (short):
We observed an eviction cascade in the per-model KV cache that, under sustained long-context batch traffic, triggered async spill failures which were not being handled by the worker. That failure path produced elevated 5xx/timeouts for requests that needed KV spill I/O.

What we did for you (completed):
- Turned on kv-spill-safety v1 which intercepts spill failures and returns the in-memory degrade path instead of failing the request; deployed at 10:00 PST.
- Increased the in-memory threshold for silverline3b for 48 hours to avoid immediate spills while we finish the fix.
- Temporarily routed your traffic to the fallback variant and added short-term concurrency limits for long-context batches.

Result: Error rate for your account fell from ~12% to <0.8% across the afternoon sampling window. You should see normal job completion for your nightly scoring run; Monica will validate job completion with your team.

Planned longer-term fixes (timeline):
- Eviction policy change + improved async spill retry semantics (engineering patch) — target: 2026-11-19 canary deploy.
- New alarms for failed-spill-rate and pending-spill-queue (in monitoring) — target: 2026-11-18.
- Postmortem and RCA doc to be shared by 2026-11-20 with concrete remediation and compensating controls.

Compensation: We will apply a credit for the impacted time window against November usage; our billing team will follow up once the window is finalized.

title: Token attribution rules for system/tool tokens — rate card confirmation
preview:
From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 09:12 PST
Subject: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the quick onboarding last week — we're getting close to finalizing the SOW but need one final piece for procurement: a clear, written explanation of how tokens are counted for our hosted usage so we can put the math in the contract. A few concrete scenarios below — can you confirm how each is billed on the hosted API (unit counts, rounding rules, and how discounts will apply):

1) System vs. user messages: We have a persistent system prompt (200 tokens) + user prompt (120 tokens). Is the system prompt always billed as input tokens every request, or is there any prefix-caching/exemption?

2) Tool/function calls: If the assistant invokes a function with arguments we send (e.g., a 300-token JSON payload) and the tool returns a 150-token structured response, do we get charged for the function-call inputs as input tokens and the returned content as output tokens?

3) Multiple small requests vs. batching: If we send two separate requests of 10 tokens each vs. a single batched request of 20 tokens, do you do rounding per request or per-batch? Any minimum per-API-call rounding behavior?

4) Streaming & partial tokens: We stream generated tokens back to the client. Does streaming change rounding, or are tokens counted as generated tokens once emitted?

5) Discounts: Our procurement team needs to know if discounts apply per-token unit price, or on the overall invoice after usage aggregation. Also, how are committed tiers applied if we go over the commitment in a given month?

I've attached a small sample usage CSV so you can show a worked example (usage_example.csv). Procurement will need this detail for the PO.

Appreciate a short written response and if possible a one-pager/example calculation we can attach to the contract.

Best,
Mark
Procurement, FinTechCo
[Attachment: usage_example.csv (csv, 3 KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 11:03 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — thanks for the clear scenarios, that helps. I've attached our current hosted rate card and a quick example calc (ratecard_v3.xlsx) and have looped in Monica (billing) and Laura (pricing) who can confirm the exact rounding/discount mechanics. Quick answers up-front:

- System prompts are billed as input on every request unless you use our prefix caching feature and the prefix is a cache hit (we can include caching rules in the SOW).
- Function/tool inputs are counted as input tokens; tool/function responses are counted as output tokens.
- Rounding is done per request (we charge integer token counts per API call) — Monica will confirm exactly how batching is handled.
- Streaming does not change semantics: tokens are counted when generated and billed as output tokens.
- Discounts: typically applied as a per-unit discount on the usage line items (i.e., token unit price is discounted once thresholds/commitments are applied). Laura will add the precedence rules for committed vs. overage pricing.

Can we set a 30-minute call tomorrow to walk through the CSV and produce the one-pager? I'm free 10–11am or 3–4pm PT.

Thanks,
Aisha
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: ratecard_v3.xlsx (xlsx, 98 KB)]

From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 15:22 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Monica from Billing here. Below are the concrete billing rules we apply for hosted API usage (I'll reference the line items in ratecard_v3.xlsx):

1) System vs. user messages
- All message content sent to the model as part of a request (system, user, assistant prefixes) is counted as input tokens for that request.
- Prefix caching: if you enable prefix caching and the prefix is a cache hit, we do not bill the cached token portion. Cache eligibility depends on exact prefix match and the model + version. We can add cache rules to the SOW.

2) Tool/Function calls
- Tool call arguments (the JSON you send as function arguments) are billed as input tokens on the request that invokes the function.
- The tool/system response returned by the function is billed as output tokens on the same API session — treated as generated output associated with that request (billed at the output rate for the model).
- If you then include the tool response in a follow-up request, that included content is billed again as input in the follow-up. There is no automatic deduplication across separate requests.

3) Batching and rounding
- Token counting is exact (integer token counts). We measure tokens per request and round up to the nearest whole token per request — there is no sub-token billing.
- If you submit multiple logical prompts in a single API call (i.e., a batch payload where you combine text), we count tokens on the combined request; rounding is applied on that combined request. Therefore, batching small prompts into a single call can avoid per-call rounding overhead.

4) Streaming
- Streaming doesn't change billing semantics: tokens are billed as output tokens as they are generated. We do not apply special per-chunk rounding; only the final generated token count is used (again rounded to whole tokens).

5) Discounts & commitments
- Discounts are applied at the per-token unit price level according to the tiering in your contract.
- Commitments: committed monthly spend reduces the effective unit price up to the committed amount. Overage above the commitment is billed at the published overage rate (which may be higher) unless you have an overage protection clause.
- Example (see ratecard_v3.xlsx): if your base unit price is $0.0008/token and you have a 10% committed-use discount up to 1B tokens, the discounted unit price for tokens within the commitment is $0.00072. Tokens above 1B are billed at the overage price.

I've attached a worked example PDF in the ratecard workbook — happy to walk through line-by-line on a call.

Thanks,
Monica Patel
Billing Lead — Redwood Inference


From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 09:10 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Thanks Monica — that's helpful. A couple follow-ups (sorry for the detail, procurement insists on edge-case clarity):

A) Deduplication / identical tool responses: If our architecture makes repeated identical function calls and the tool returns identical content (e.g., cached downstream microservice returns "OK"), does Redwood deduplicate identical generated outputs across requests or still bill each generate?

B) External fetches inside function args: We sometimes pass a short pointer to a large payload that the function runtime fetches externally (the argu- ment is just a URL + metadata, small token footprint). The fetched content is not included in the original request tokenization — do you bill the fetched content if it is materialized into the function response?

C) Sample numbers: Could you show a quick worked example for the following: system prompt 200 tokens + user 120 tokens + function args 300 tokens triggering a 150-token function response + final assistant reply 80 tokens. Show token counts (input vs output), rounding, and unit price math for the first 10,000 requests with a 10% commitment discount up to 1M tokens.

If you can post that in the thread (or in a short PDF), procurement can finish the PO. Also we prefer a single page that we can attach to the SOW explaining rounding and discount application.

Thanks,
Mark


From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 12:30 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Laura from Pricing. Short answers on commercial mechanics, and I'll attach a scenario calc to the HubSpot deal (link below).

- Deduplication: We do not deduplicate across distinct API calls. Each request that results in generated output is billed for that generated content. If you rely on cache hits at the prefix level (see Monica's note), that may avoid billing for the cached portion, but identical outputs from separate calls are billed separately.

- External fetches: If the function runtime fetches external payloads and returns content in the function response that is then emitted by the model, that generated content is billed as output tokens. The fact that the original request had a URL pointer does not change that — the billing is based on what was sent to/from the model at tokenization time and what the model generated.

- Requested worked example (summary):
  * Per-request tokenization:
    - Input tokens: system (200) + user (120) + function args (300) = 620 input tokens billed on the invoking request.
    - Output tokens: function response (150) + final assistant reply (80) = 230 output tokens billed (these are generated output associated with the session).
  * Per-request rounding: tokens are whole integers; we don't inflate beyond the actual token counts except rounding up to the nearest integer (which in practice is a no-op since token counts are integers).
  * Unit price math (example): base input unit price = $0.0004/token; base output unit price = $0.0008/token. With a 10% commitment discount up to 1,000,000 tokens, discounted input price = $0.00036; discounted output price = $0.00072 for tokens within commitment.

  Example calc for 10,000 identical requests:
    - Total input tokens = 620 * 10,000 = 6,200,000 tokens
    - Total output tokens = 230 * 10,000 = 2,300,000 tokens
    - Tokens covered by 1,000,000-token commitment (applies to whichever line is specified in your contract — typically total tokens combined or broken out by type; we can make it per total tokens if you prefer): assuming commitment applies to combined tokens and is consumed first, 1,000,000 tokens get discounted. The remaining tokens billed at overage rates.

I'll drop a short PDF to the HubSpot deal and to this thread with the line-by-line numbers; Laura (pricing) note: exact precedence (whether commitment consumes input vs output first) is negotiable and should be specified in the contract; defaults are in the attached ratecard.

Link: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567 (worked example uploaded)

Cheers,
Laura Bennett
Pricing Manager — Redwood Inference

text:
From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 09:12 PST
Subject: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the quick onboarding last week — we're getting close to finalizing the SOW but need one final piece for procurement: a clear, written explanation of how tokens are counted for our hosted usage so we can put the math in the contract. A few concrete scenarios below — can you confirm how each is billed on the hosted API (unit counts, rounding rules, and how discounts will apply):

1) System vs. user messages: We have a persistent system prompt (200 tokens) + user prompt (120 tokens). Is the system prompt always billed as input tokens every request, or is there any prefix-caching/exemption?

2) Tool/function calls: If the assistant invokes a function with arguments we send (e.g., a 300-token JSON payload) and the tool returns a 150-token structured response, do we get charged for the function-call inputs as input tokens and the returned content as output tokens?

3) Multiple small requests vs. batching: If we send two separate requests of 10 tokens each vs. a single batched request of 20 tokens, do you do rounding per request or per-batch? Any minimum per-API-call rounding behavior?

4) Streaming & partial tokens: We stream generated tokens back to the client. Does streaming change rounding, or are tokens counted as generated tokens once emitted?

5) Discounts: Our procurement team needs to know if discounts apply per-token unit price, or on the overall invoice after usage aggregation. Also, how are committed tiers applied if we go over the commitment in a given month?

I've attached a small sample usage CSV so you can show a worked example (usage_example.csv). Procurement will need this detail for the PO.

Appreciate a short written response and if possible a one-pager/example calculation we can attach to the contract.

Best,
Mark
Procurement, FinTechCo
[Attachment: usage_example.csv (csv, 3 KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 11:03 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — thanks for the clear scenarios, that helps. I've attached our current hosted rate card and a quick example calc (ratecard_v3.xlsx) and have looped in Monica (billing) and Laura (pricing) who can confirm the exact rounding/discount mechanics. Quick answers up-front:

- System prompts are billed as input on every request unless you use our prefix caching feature and the prefix is a cache hit (we can include caching rules in the SOW).
- Function/tool inputs are counted as input tokens; tool/function responses are counted as output tokens.
- Rounding is done per request (we charge integer token counts per API call) — Monica will confirm exactly how batching is handled.
- Streaming does not change semantics: tokens are counted when generated and billed as output tokens.
- Discounts: typically applied as a per-unit discount on the usage line items (i.e., token unit price is discounted once thresholds/commitments are applied). Laura will add the precedence rules for committed vs. overage pricing.

Can we set a 30-minute call tomorrow to walk through the CSV and produce the one-pager? I'm free 10–11am or 3–4pm PT.

Thanks,
Aisha
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: ratecard_v3.xlsx (xlsx, 98 KB)]

From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 15:22 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Monica from Billing here. Below are the concrete billing rules we apply for hosted API usage (I'll reference the line items in ratecard_v3.xlsx):

1) System vs. user messages
- All message content sent to the model as part of a request (system, user, assistant prefixes) is counted as input tokens for that request.
- Prefix caching: if you enable prefix caching and the prefix is a cache hit, we do not bill the cached token portion. Cache eligibility depends on exact prefix match and the model + version. We can add cache rules to the SOW.

2) Tool/Function calls
- Tool call arguments (the JSON you send as function arguments) are billed as input tokens on the request that invokes the function.
- The tool/system response returned by the function is billed as output tokens on the same API session — treated as generated output associated with that request (billed at the output rate for the model).
- If you then include the tool response in a follow-up request, that included content is billed again as input in the follow-up. There is no automatic deduplication across separate requests.

3) Batching and rounding
- Token counting is exact (integer token counts). We measure tokens per request and round up to the nearest whole token per request — there is no sub-token billing.
- If you submit multiple logical prompts in a single API call (i.e., a batch payload where you combine text), we count tokens on the combined request; rounding is applied on that combined request. Therefore, batching small prompts into a single call can avoid per-call rounding overhead.

4) Streaming
- Streaming doesn't change billing semantics: tokens are billed as output tokens as they are generated. We do not apply special per-chunk rounding; only the final generated token count is used (again rounded to whole tokens).

5) Discounts & commitments
- Discounts are applied at the per-token unit price level according to the tiering in your contract.
- Commitments: committed monthly spend reduces the effective unit price up to the committed amount. Overage above the commitment is billed at the published overage rate (which may be higher) unless you have an overage protection clause.
- Example (see ratecard_v3.xlsx): if your base unit price is $0.0008/token and you have a 10% committed-use discount up to 1B tokens, the discounted unit price for tokens within the commitment is $0.00072. Tokens above 1B are billed at the overage price.

I've attached a worked example PDF in the ratecard workbook — happy to walk through line-by-line on a call.

Thanks,
Monica Patel
Billing Lead — Redwood Inference


From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 09:10 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Thanks Monica — that's helpful. A couple follow-ups (sorry for the detail, procurement insists on edge-case clarity):

A) Deduplication / identical tool responses: If our architecture makes repeated identical function calls and the tool returns identical content (e.g., cached downstream microservice returns "OK"), does Redwood deduplicate identical generated outputs across requests or still bill each generate?

B) External fetches inside function args: We sometimes pass a short pointer to a large payload that the function runtime fetches externally (the argu- ment is just a URL + metadata, small token footprint). The fetched content is not included in the original request tokenization — do you bill the fetched content if it is materialized into the function response?

C) Sample numbers: Could you show a quick worked example for the following: system prompt 200 tokens + user 120 tokens + function args 300 tokens triggering a 150-token function response + final assistant reply 80 tokens. Show token counts (input vs output), rounding, and unit price math for the first 10,000 requests with a 10% commitment discount up to 1M tokens.

If you can post that in the thread (or in a short PDF), procurement can finish the PO. Also we prefer a single page that we can attach to the SOW explaining rounding and discount application.

Thanks,
Mark


From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 12:30 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Laura from Pricing. Short answers on commercial mechanics, and I'll attach a scenario calc to the HubSpot deal (link below).

- Deduplication: We do not deduplicate across distinct API calls. Each request that results in generated output is billed for that generated content. If you rely on cache hits at the prefix level (see Monica's note), that may avoid billing for the cached portion, but identical outputs from separate calls are billed separately.

- External fetches: If the function runtime fetches external payloads and returns content in the function response that is then emitted by the model, that generated content is billed as output tokens. The fact that the original request had a URL pointer does not change that — the billing is based on what was sent to/from the model at tokenization time and what the model generated.

- Requested worked example (summary):
  * Per-request tokenization:
    - Input tokens: system (200) + user (120) + function args (300) = 620 input tokens billed on the invoking request.
    - Output tokens: function response (150) + final assistant reply (80) = 230 output tokens billed (these are generated output associated with the session).
  * Per-request rounding: tokens are whole integers; we don't inflate beyond the actual token counts except rounding up to the nearest integer (which in practice is a no-op since token counts are integers).
  * Unit price math (example): base input unit price = $0.0004/token; base output unit price = $0.0008/token. With a 10% commitment discount up to 1,000,000 tokens, discounted input price = $0.00036; discounted output price = $0.00072 for tokens within commitment.

  Example calc for 10,000 identical requests:
    - Total input tokens = 620 * 10,000 = 6,200,000 tokens
    - Total output tokens = 230 * 10,000 = 2,300,000 tokens
    - Tokens covered by 1,000,000-token commitment (applies to whichever line is specified in your contract — typically total tokens combined or broken out by type; we can make it per total tokens if you prefer): assuming commitment applies to combined tokens and is consumed first, 1,000,000 tokens get discounted. The remaining tokens billed at overage rates.

I'll drop a short PDF to the HubSpot deal and to this thread with the line-by-line numbers; Laura (pricing) note: exact precedence (whether commitment consumes input vs output first) is negotiable and should be specified in the contract; defaults are in the attached ratecard.

Link: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567 (worked example uploaded)

Cheers,
Laura Bennett
Pricing Manager — Redwood Inference

title: Nested remit waterfall for multi-subsidiary invoicing — proposal and next steps
preview:
From: Miriam Shaw <miriam.shaw@helixtx.com>

Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helixtx.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 15:02:00 -0500
Subject: Multi-entity invoicing — remit routing & VAT handling

Hi Karthik,

Hope you’re well. We’re finalizing our vendor onboarding for Q1 and need to confirm how Redwood will handle invoices and remittances across our legal entities. We have three bill-to entities (Helix US, Helix EU, Helix APAC) and a mix of central cost centers. Specific asks:

- Can Redwood issue a single customer-facing invoice that splits line items to the three entities for accounting?
- Will the invoice show VAT per EU entity and include VAT IDs on-line level?
- We need the ability to map seat counts to cost centers so our internal chargebacks can post correctly.
- Is there a recommended flow for remittance (one payment to Redwood with allocations versus separate payments)?

We’re also sensitive to PO/contract signatures and would prefer a sample split-invoice layout we can circulate with Accounting and Procurement. Could you point us to a template or a short proposal?

Thanks,
Miriam
Miriam Shaw
Head of Procurement
Helix Therapeutics

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Miriam Shaw <miriam.shaw@helixtx.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Nov 2026 09:18:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Multi-entity invoicing — remit routing & VAT handling

Hi Miriam — thanks for the clear list. TL;DR: yes, we can support a single invoice with per-line entity and VAT breakdowns and provide CSV/line-level mappings for your chargeback process. Details and a short proposal below.

Proposed approach:
- Single consolidated invoice PDF for your AP team that contains: per-line legal entity allocation, VAT ID shown for EU lines, and a per-line cost center tag.
- Remittance options: (A) one payment to Redwood with an accompanying allocation file we apply (preferred operationally), or (B) separate payments per legal entity. We recommend (A) with a nested remit waterfall so your treasury makes one payment and we settle internally across our ledgers.
- We will supply a sample split invoice (xlsx) and a brief remit waterfall proposal for review.

Next steps we propose:
1) Share your legal entity names + tax IDs (if you’re comfortable);
2) We’ll send a sample invoice + a short PDF describing the nested remit waterfall and the processing SLA;
3) Procurement/Legal review DocuSign SOW/PO if needed — we can prepare the DocuSign-ready SOW with the split-invoice appendix.

Looping Ben (payments) and Laura (billing ops) so they can weigh in on ledger tagging and timing.

Best,
Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
--

- Ledger tagging: we'll include a 'bill_entity' and 'charge_costcenter' field at line level. That will map to our accounts receivable posting and to your incoming AR mapping.
- Nested remit waterfall: sequence = (1) apply centralized payment to primary legal entity (Redwood US), (2) internal settlement to Redwood EU / APAC ledgers using intercompany offsets, (3) VAT reclaim handling where applicable.
- Timing: If you choose single-payment flow (our recommendation), funds posted T+1 to primary ledger; intercompany settlement runs T+2–T+3 depending on currency rails.
- Audit artifacts: we’ll include an allocation CSV and a PDF invoice with footnote traceability.

I attached a short proposal doc (nested_remit_proposal.pdf) and a sample invoice layout. Want to make sure we call out VAT reverse-charge options for Helix EU if they qualify.

Ben

Redwood Inference

Thanks Riley — got the resume and quick write-up. Loop planning notes below (draft agenda attached).

High-level ask for internal team: please confirm (a) whether you can join the assigned slot, (b) any timezone constraints, and (c) whether you want to swap ordering for context continuity. Proposed day-of sequencing (draft):
- Slot 1 (45m): System / infra architecture — Marcus (lead) + 2
- Slot 2 (45m): Model infra & runtime ops — Tessa + 1
- Slot 3 (30m): Coding/debugging exercise (pair via Collab) — Marcus + 1
- Slot 4 (30m): Hiring manager + culture / cross-team fit — Laura (HM)

Buffers: 10 mins between slots; 30 mins after final slot for formal debrief.

Debrief coverage: Laura to capture hiring manager notes; Marcus to draft technical scorecard; Neha to consolidate final recommendation and post to Greenhouse.

Attachments: draft agenda + simple interviewer matrix.

[Attachment: Loop_Agenda_v1.docx]

Chloe

Chloe / team — I can take the system-design panel and lead the technical scorecard. A couple thoughts after reading the agenda:

- Swap Slot 2 and Slot 3 (model infra before coding). Reason: model infra discussion seeds the coding prompt and reduces context ramp for candidate.
- Slot lengths: happy with 45 / 45 / 30 / 30. Can we make the coding slot 40 minutes (30 coding + 10 quick follow-up) so we keep the final debrief timing reasonable?
- Availability: I have earliest availability between 8:30–11:30am PT on 11/24; after that I have conflicts.
- Debrief: I'm fine to own the tech doc; prefer a 30-min dedicated debrief immediately following the loop rather than asynchronous notes. If we must, we can do quick 10-min syncs after each slot for immediate red flags and then a 30-min final.

Marcus

title: Fwd: Notice of Alleged IP Infringement — ImageSynth outputs
preview:
From: Elena Morales <elena@spartacus.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-13T09:22:00-08:00
Subject: Notice of Alleged IP Infringement: ImageSynth

Ben —

Attached is a formal notice from our legal team alleging that several outputs produced by your API were substantially similar to copyrighted illustrations owned by Atlas Creative. We're sending this as the designated contact per our contract. The notice includes three representative outputs (attached) and an explanation of why Atlas believes the content infringes.

Requested next steps: please confirm receipt within 72 hours, preserve all request/response logs for the session IDs listed below, and provide a point of contact for your legal and engineering teams. We would like to avoid escalation, so a proactive remediation or attribution approach would be appreciated.

Sample session IDs: s-122334, s-122335, s-122339
Attached: claim_letter_spartacus.pdf, sample_output_1.txt

Thanks,
Elena Morales
Head of Legal Operations

> - Complete vendor setup in Netsuite
> - Create vendor record and attach W9
> - Configure payment methods: ACH + Wire (both enabled)
> - Enter initial PO-2026-1189 and queue wire transfer upon approval
> 
> If you need the PO to be signed before the wire, confirm and we will expedite internal approvals.
>

Thanks again for a productive call this morning, Alicia — quick recap and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth to share with your team.

1) High-level success criteria (proposed)
- Latency: 95th pctile < 120ms for inference in your app flow

- Week 0 (this week): finalize access and procurement contacts

- Procurement & contract: Novalabs Procurement (Marco / contracts@novalabs.io)

4) Blockers & asks from Novalabs
- Confirm procurement lead/contact and expected procurement timeline
- Provide staging VPC CIDR and any firewall constraints
- Send your evaluation test set or sample queries for the quality threshold

Attachments: trial_success_criteria.xlsx (xlsx), trial_milestones.pdf (pdf)

- Alicia: confirm procurement contact + estimated procurement SLA by EOD Monday

title: PaperTrail expansion & renewal — post-call recap and next steps
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Olivia Zhang <olivia@papertrail.ai>, Rajiv Mehta <rajiv@papertrail.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 15:08:00 -0800
Subject: PaperTrail expansion & renewal — post-call recap and next steps

Thanks again for taking the time earlier today. Quick recap of what we discussed and proposed next steps so everyone has a single source of truth. Please call out any corrections.

What we heard (pain + goals)
- Peak latency spikes during nightly ingestion windows (95th percentile hits ~1.2s) impacting index refresh and downstream search quality.
- Overall token spend has grown 3.5x year over year; team is looking to contain unit costs without sacrificing response quality.
- New workloads: (1) higher-concurrency RAG for customer support (short context, low-latency), (2) nightly batch embeddings job for updated knowledge base, and (3) a small real-time summarization endpoint for internal QA.
- Security/compliance: PaperTrail will need SOC 2 evidence + logs retention options for audit next quarter.

Redwood's recommended starting point
1) Short-term: run a 30-day Dedicated pilot (reserved capacity pool) sized for current 95th percentile concurrency with burst to handle nightly jobs. This reduces per-token costs and stabilizes latency.
2) Tech optimizations during pilot: KV/prefix caching for RAG, mixed-precision quantization on the embeddings model, and request-level batching for the nightly job.
3) Parallel path: have Legal/Procurement start procurement checklist while SEs run technical sizing and a Deep Technical Review (DTR).

Action items & owners (proposed)
- Aisha: Deliver the usage export and initial sizing recommendation (attached) — today.
- Grace (SE): Run a technical deep-dive and pilot plan (45–60 min) — week of Nov 16.
- Ben (AE): Prepare commercial outline for a 6‑month Dedicated reservation, with optional burst capacity and a preferred renewal discount — by Nov 18.
- Olivia / Rajiv: Share the list of internal stakeholders for security review and confirm target procurement timeline (expected Nov 30 internal approval).

Attachments shared in this email:
- papertrail_usage_nov2026.csv (usage export)
- rtm_sizing_recommendation.pdf (initial sizing + expected latency/cost impact)
- mutual_action_plan_PaperTrail.xlsx (M.A.P. draft)

Next meetings
- Tech deep-dive w/ Grace: proposing Tue, Nov 17 10:00–11:00 PT (I can send a calendar invite once you confirm).
- Commercial sync (ben + procurement): Thu, Nov 19 09:00–09:30 PT.

If that aligns, I will: (a) lock the two meeting times, (b) share a HubSpot deal link and the MAP in Drive, and (c) start routing SOC2 pieces to your security contact.

Thanks — looking forward to moving this forward.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
https://redwoodinference.com

--
Attachments: papertrail_usage_nov2026.csv (21 KB), rtm_sizing_recommendation.pdf (320 KB), mutual_action_plan_PaperTrail.xlsx (48 KB)

From: Olivia Zhang <olivia@papertrail.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rajiv Mehta <rajiv@papertrail.ai>
Date: Fri, 13 Nov 2026 09:24:00 -0800
Subject: Re: PaperTrail expansion & renewal — post-call recap and next steps

Thanks Aisha — this is a great summary and mostly matches our notes. A couple quick clarifications and asks: 

- Concurrency: nightly ingestion concurrency was overstated on our side earlier. 95th percentile concurrent requests for the nightly job is closer to 220 (not 400) — this lowers the short-term Dedicated sizing you outlined.
- Security: yes, SOC 2 evidence is required. Additionally, our procurement team will want to confirm data residency controls (logs retained for 12 months) and any support SLAs around incident response for production inference. I'll loop Legal/Procurement once we have the commercial outline.
- New workload priority: RAG (customer support) > embeddings (nightly) > summarization. We want the pilot to demonstrate consistent sub-200ms median latency for the RAG path under realistic load.

I attached our internal procurement checklist (procurement_checklist_PT.pdf) — it's not exhaustive but should help Ben with commercial framing. Rajiv and I are available for the tech deep-dive on Tue Nov 17; either 10:00 or 11:30 PT works.

On the pilot timeline, is a 30-day pilot still the recommendation, or would Redwood prefer an initial 14-day proof-of-concept followed by a 30-day ramp? We want to minimize interruption to our production ingestion.

Appreciate the quick turnaround here.

Best,
Olivia Zhang
Head of Platform Engineering | PaperTrail AI

---
Attachment: procurement_checklist_PT.pdf (14 KB)

On Thu, Nov 12, 2026 at 3:08 PM Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Thanks again for taking the time earlier today. Quick recap of what we discussed...
>
> [message trimmed]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Olivia Zhang <olivia@papertrail.ai>, Rajiv Mehta <rajiv@papertrail.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>, Legal <legal@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 10:24:00 -0800
Subject: Re: PaperTrail expansion & renewal — post-call recap and next steps

Appreciate the clarification, Olivia — noted concurrency correction. Quick follow-ups and firmed-up plan below.

Updated sizing & pilot approach
- Given the corrected 220 concurrency for the nightly job and the RAG priority, Grace will size the Dedicated pilot to cover 250 concurrent inference workers (gives ~15% headroom) and configure a separate smaller pool for nightly batch embeddings to mitigate latency interference.
- We'll run a 14-day technical PoC (no billing change) to validate configurations and latency targets, then convert to a 30-day reserved pilot if the PoC hits the agreed KPIs. This shortens your exposure window while giving us data to size reservations.

Confirmed action owners & dates
- Aisha: upload usage export and MAP to Drive; created the HubSpot deal and linked in this thread — DONE.
- Grace: deliver DTR + recommended pilot config (by Tue, Nov 17 close of business).
- Ben: commercial outline for the 6‑month reservation + procurement checklist responses (by Thu, Nov 18).
- Olivia/Rajiv: confirm final list of stakeholders for security evidence and target date for procurement sign-off (by Fri, Nov 20).

Links & artifacts
- MAP (Drive): https://drive.google.com/open?id=1A2b3Cmutual_action_plan
- HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567/DEAL-PT-2026-11
- SOW draft (DocuSign): https://app.docusign.com/packets/XYZ-PT-SOW

Next steps
1) Grace will send a calendar invite for DTR on Tue Nov 17 10:00 PT (we'll keep the 11:30 slot tentative).
2) Ben will post a commercial outline in the HubSpot deal and drop a summarized deck in Drive for procurement.
3) Aisha will coordinate SOC2 evidence handoff to your security point once Olivia confirms the contact.

If that looks good I'll go ahead and schedule the DTR and the commercial sync.

Thanks everyone — excited to get this moving.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

--
Attachments referenced earlier: papertrail_usage_nov2026.csv, rtm_sizing_recommendation.pdf, mutual_action_plan_PaperTrail.xlsx

Updated sizing & pilot approach
- Given the corrected 220 concurrency for the nightly job and the RAG priority, Grace will size the Dedicated pilot to cover 250 concurrent inference workers (gives ~15% headroom) and configure a separate smaller pool for nightly batch embeddings to mitigate latency interference.
- We'll run a 14-day technical PoC (no billing change) to validate configurations and latency targets, then convert to a 30-day reserved pilot if the PoC hits the agreed KPIs. This shortens your exposure window while giving us data to size reservations.

Confirmed action owners & dates
- Aisha: upload usage export and MAP to Drive; created the HubSpot deal and linked in this thread — DONE.
- Grace: deliver DTR + recommended pilot config (by Tue, Nov 17 close of business).
- Ben: commercial outline for the 6‑month reservation + procurement checklist responses (by Thu, Nov 18).
- Olivia/Rajiv: confirm final list of stakeholders for security evidence and target date for procurement sign-off (by Fri, Nov 20).

Links & artifacts
- MAP (Drive): https://drive.google.com/open?id=1A2b3Cmutual_action_plan
- HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567/DEAL-PT-2026-11
- SOW draft (DocuSign): https://app.docusign.com/packets/XYZ-PT-SOW

Next steps
1) Grace will send a calendar invite for DTR on Tue Nov 17 10:00 PT (we'll keep the 11:30 slot tentative).
2) Ben will post a commercial outline in the HubSpot deal and drop a summarized deck in Drive for procurement.
3) Aisha will coordinate SOC2 evidence handoff to your security point once Olivia confirms the contact.

If that looks good I'll go ahead and schedule the DTR and the commercial sync.

Thanks everyone — excited to get this moving.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

title: LiftHealth MAP: unit economics sanity check and simulation plan
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.example>
To: Eva Morales <eva@lifthealth.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.example>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.example>
Cc: ops@lifthealth.com
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: LiftHealth MAP: unit economics sanity check and simulation plan

Hi Eva / LiftHealth ops —

Thanks again for the demo last week. Per our MAP milestone 2 (evaluation + cost acceptance), we want to run a short sanity pass on tokens-per-dollar and the impact of caching/prefetch/batching assumptions before signoff. I attached the MAP_cost_model_v1.xlsx that has our starting assumptions. High-level asks: 

- Can you confirm the expected average prompt length (tokens) for the 3 primary flows we discussed (intake form -> summary, longitudinal notes, and triage chat)?
- Any L7+ SLAs we need to account for that will limit batching windows?
- Are you comfortable allowing a small prefix cache (30s TTL) for repeated patient contexts during a session?

What we'll do on our side this week:
- Run a 24h prefetched-cache simulation (prefetch-sim-config.yml attached) to show tokens/$ with and without prefix caching

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.example>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.example>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.example>, Eva Morales <eva@lifthealth.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Nov 2026 08:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: LiftHealth MAP: unit economics sanity check and simulation plan

Thanks for the data points, Eva. A few technical clarifications and my proposed sim matrix: 

- Baseline model: small-llm-v2 (quantized 4-bit) — tokens/sec and KV cache behavior in our runtime match the assumptions in MAP_cost_model_v1.
- For triage chat (p95 <= 1.2s) we should assume batching=1 for that flow in cost calc. For the summary pipeline we can test batching of 4 / 8 / 16.
- Prefix caching: simulate TTLs: none, 30s, 90s. Cache hit multiplier: 0.6 tokens (i.e., cached prefix reduces token decode steps by ~40% for repeated prompts). We'll include a churn scenario where session switches user context every 45s to show eviction costs.
- Prefetch vs on-demand: run two variants — prefetching top-2 likely next prompts (adds small read cost but reduces latency) vs purely on-demand.

Proposed sim matrix (24h synthetic traffic):
- Flow A (triage chat): batching=1, cache TTLs: none/30s/90s, request rate 600 reqs/hr
- Flow B (summary): batching=1/4/8/16, request rate 120 reqs/hr (can be higher off-hours)
- Flow C (longitudinal notes): batching=1/4, request rate 80 reqs/hr, median prompt 280 tokens

Deliverables: per-flow tokens/request, tokens/$ (hosted), variance with 95% CI, top-3 worst-case overruns. I'll run sims Thu morning and drop results into the Drive link Ben included before the 11:00 sync.

Notes on financial gating: if the tokens/$ at expected traffic exceed the MAP threshold in the SOW by >20%, we'll prepare two remediation proposals: (A) quantization/alternative model variant + prefetch tuning; (B) committed Dedicated pool sizing to hit unit cost targets.

-Karthik

title: Intro / Demo intake — clarifying traffic profile and preferred week for live demo
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Priya Nambiar <priya@parcelpro.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Intro / Demo intake — clarifying traffic profile and preferred week for live demo

Hi Priya —

Thanks for signing up and trying Redwood. I’d love to set up a short 45-minute demo to walk through how teams like yours get predictable latency and cost on high-volume generation workloads. Before we lock a slot, a few quick intake questions so we tailor the session and bring the right folks: 

- Primary use case: (e.g., customer support triage, document parsing, label text generation)
- Expected monthly request volume and typical concurrent requests at peak
- Average tokens per request and whether there are longer context windows (>=1k tokens)
- Timeline for a POC / pilot (weeks/months) and key success criteria
- Any data sensitivity or compliance needs (PII, regulated data, SOC2 requirements)
- Who from your side should attend (product, eng, security)?

Proposed demo windows next week (all PT):
- Wed Nov 18 — 10:00–10:45am PT
- Wed Nov 18 — 2:00–2:45pm PT
- Thu Nov 19 — 9:00–9:45am PT

I’m attaching our short demo checklist so you know what we’ll cover. If you reply with the answers above and a preferred slot we’ll send a calendar invite and invite Ben (AE) to cover procurement questions.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Sales Development Rep, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachment: Redwood-demo-checklist.pdf (PDF, 120KB)


From: Priya Nambiar <priya@parcelpro.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Nov 2026 09:46:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Intro / Demo intake — clarifying traffic profile and preferred week for live demo

Thanks — happy to take the 45m walkthrough. Quick answers below: 

- Use case: label text generation for printed shipping labels + automated first-line support reply drafts for CS agents.
- Monthly volume: ~200k generation requests/month today (expected to grow 2–3x over next 6 months).
- Peak concurrency: we see spikes up to ~60 requests/sec during morning cutover windows.

- Timeline: target POC in ~6 weeks; decision target is end of Q1.
- Data sensitivity: contains PII (names & addresses) and order metadata — need to discuss retention/retention controls.
- Attendees: myself (Product), Rajesh Kumar (backend lead, rajesh@parcelpro.com), and our security engineer Mira Shah (mira@parcelpro.com).

title: Prioritize: BYOK + SSO audit intake — sprint asks & exception gating
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Cc: Alice Hunt <alice.hunt@medispring.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Intake: MediSpring BYOK + SSO audit asks — need prioritization + exception path

Team — forwarding a new intake from MediSpring (attached). High level: they
are asking for a short retention window (30d) for some PII-derived tokens,
BYOK with customer-provided CMKs, and SSO audit exports that include per-token
-> user mapping. Their procurement window is tight (renewal on 2027-01-15),
and security has flagged a blocking question around our current keystore
rotation/ephemeral handling.

Attachments: medispring_evidence_request_20261110.pdf (pdf), initial-ask-summary.csv (csv)

Asks:
- Marissa: can product slot a quick intake in next sprint to scope changes?
- Jonas: rough tech feasibility + blockers for BYOK + per-token SSO mapping (estimate T-shirt S/M/L)
- Priyom: legal/ops gate — what minimum artifacts we must produce for evidence and how exception gating works for short windows

My proposal: triage today, decide whether we can accept a temporary exception (short-window) with a compensating control (audit snapshot export + escrowed key). If we can’t meet the ask, propose fallback options to MediSpring before their procurement deadline.

I’ll set a 30-min bridgecall if you want to loop the customer. Please reply with your earliest availability and initial sizing.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 415 555 0198

---
Forwarded attachment stubs: medispring_evidence_request_20261110.pdf (11 pages), initial-ask-summary.csv (4 rows)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Intake: MediSpring BYOK + SSO audit asks — need prioritization + exception path

Thanks Ben — quick read. Two clarifying requests from product side:
1) When they say \"per-token -> user mapping\" for SSO audit exports, do they require that mapping to be re-creatable for any token issued historically, or just for tokens minted in the last 30 days?
2) For BYOK: do they require that the CMK never leaves their KMS (true BYOK) or is a customer-supplied key that we import acceptable?

My suggestion: create a short intake doc and a mutual action plan (MAP) with three lanes: \"Must-have to close\", \"Can be delivered as exception with compensating control\", \"Longer-term roadmap\". If we decide an exception is required, we should define an explicit sunset (example: exception valid until product ships escrowed-keystore flow).

I can take point on the intake doc and MAP. Jonas — can you provide a feasibility note by EOD tomorrow (S/M/L + blocking infra changes)? Priyom — can you flag the legal/ops minimum evidence artifacts so I can call them out in the MAP?

Marissa
Marissa Cole
Director, Product — Security Integrations
marissa.cole@redwood.com

> Team — forwarding a new intake from MediSpring (attached). High level: they
> are asking for a short retention window (30d) for some PII-derived tokens,
> BYOK with customer-provided CMKs, and SSO audit exports that include per-token
> -> user mapping. Their procurement window is tight (renewal on 2027-01-15),
> and security has flagged a blocking question around our current keystore
> rotation/ephemeral handling.

> - Marissa: can product slot a quick intake in next sprint to scope changes?
> - Jonas: rough tech feasibility + blockers for BYOK + per-token SSO mapping (estimate T-shirt S/M/L)
> - Priyom: legal/ops gate — what minimum artifacts we must produce for evidence and how exception gating works for short windows
>
> My proposal: triage today, decide whether we can accept a temporary exception (short-window) with a compensating control (audit snapshot export + escrowed key). If we can’t meet the ask, propose fallback options to MediSpring before their procurement deadline.
>
> I’ll set a 30-min bridgecall if you want to loop the customer. Please reply with your earliest availability and initial sizing.
>
> Thanks,
> Ben


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Nov 2026 14:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Intake: MediSpring BYOK + SSO audit asks — need prioritization + exception path

Marissa / Ben — quick feasibility note. High level:
- BYOK (customer-managed CMKs): MEDIUM. We support customer-supplied keys via our keystore interface, but full-on BYOK where the CMK never leaves customer KMS requires HSM handoff and additional provisioning work (ops + onboarding). Estimated work: ~3-4 sprints to make this self-serve; 1 sprint to deliver a manual/onboarding path with Ops assistance.
- Short retention window (30d) for token metadata: EASY. Our retention config already supports per-tenant retention TTL for some telemetry; we need to add per-token-type TTL and a small schema migration. Est: 1 sprint (S).
- Per-token -> user mapping in SSO audit export: HARD. Right now we record token issuance events with a token id and subject on issuance, but the long-retention of that mapping is tied to our default audit retention. To guarantee re-constructability historically we either: a) extend the audit retention (cost), b) store a snapshot export (compensating control), or c) use deterministic token user-hash references allowed by MediSpring. Implementing historical full mapping with search & export is ~2 sprints plus infra for query exports (M).

Proposed immediate plan (triage):
1) Ship per-token TTL in next sprint (low risk) — gives immediate product parity for short-window requirement.
2) Offer manual BYOK onboarding with Ops (documented runbook) as a near-term alternative while we plan self-serve BYOK (ops-runbook -> 1 sprint to deliver).
3) For historical per-token mapping, offer scheduled daily audit snapshots (S3 exports) and escrowed key access for the snapshot. That provides MediSpring with the mapping without enlarging our online retention.

I linked an internal RFC (SEC-721) with more detail: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/SEC-721

If we go with the snapshot + escrow approach, product effort is small and we can treat the rest as roadmap work. I can draft a short MAP entry with these three lanes.

-Jonas

Marissa / Ben — quick feasibility note. High level:
- BYOK (customer-managed CMKs): MEDIUM. We support customer-supplied keys via our keystore interface, but full-on BYOK where the CMK never leaves customer KMS requires HSM handoff and additional provisioning work (ops + onboarding). Estimated work: ~3-4 sprints to make this self-serve; 1 sprint to deliver a manual/onboarding path with Ops assistance.
- Short retention window (30d) for token metadata: EASY. Our retention config already supports per-tenant retention TTL for some telemetry; we need to add per-token-type TTL and a small schema migration. Est: 1 sprint (S).
- Per-token -> user mapping in SSO audit export: HARD. Right now we record token issuance events with a token id and subject on issuance, but the long-retention of that mapping is tied to our default audit retention. To guarantee re-constructability historically we either: a) extend the audit retention (cost), b) store a snapshot export (compensating control), or c) use deterministic token user-hash references allowed by MediSpring. Implementing historical full mapping with search & export is ~2 sprints plus infra for query exports (M).

Proposed immediate plan (triage):
1) Ship per-token TTL in next sprint (low risk) — gives immediate product parity for short-window requirement.
2) Offer manual BYOK onboarding with Ops (documented runbook) as a near-term alternative while we plan self-serve BYOK (ops-runbook -> 1 sprint to deliver).
3) For historical per-token mapping, offer scheduled daily audit snapshots (S3 exports) and escrowed key access for the snapshot. That provides MediSpring with the mapping without enlarging our online retention.

I linked an internal RFC (SEC-721) with more detail: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/SEC-721

If we go with the snapshot + escrow approach, product effort is small and we can treat the rest as roadmap work. I can draft a short MAP entry with these three lanes.

-Jonas
