title: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam@novacorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@novacorp.com
Date: 2026-11-12T09:18:00-08:00
Subject: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Ben —

Thanks for the demo last week. Procurement here at Novacorp asked me to follow up with a couple of contract-level items before we can finalize the order form. The two big items are: indemnity scope for third-party IP claims tied to model output, and how evidence will be produced/retained if a claim is raised.

High-level asks (from our side):
- Confirmation that Redwood will indemnify for third-party IP claims attributable to model output used by our customers (we're specifically concerned about output that reproduces third-party proprietary content).
- Clarify whether Redwood or Novacorp controls defense / settlement decisions.
- Outline what evidence (logs, prompt history, training provenance) Redwood will provide and expected timelines (we typically ask for evidence within 30 days of notice).
- Any standard carveouts (training data, open-source model components, downstream misuse).

I've attached our procurement questionnaire with the redline requests. Can you loop in legal and share a suggested compromise? Procurement is on a tight cadence — would appreciate a proposed approach by end of week.

Thanks,
Liam O'Connor
Senior Procurement Manager
Novacorp
Attachments: Novacorp_Procurement_Q.pdf (application/pdf)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam@novacorp.com>
Cc: procurement@novacorp.com, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-12T11:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Liam —

Appreciate the clear ask and the questionnaire. Looping in Kimberly (legal) and Marissa (account lead) to propose workable language. Our immediate preference is to narrow the indemnity to third-party IP claims tied to our hosted outputs and to include cooperation/notice timelines rather than an unconstrained defense obligation.

Quick question for procurement: do you need a blanket defense-control carveout (customer to control) or would a compromise where Redwood retains initial defense but Novacorp may step in with cause work? That tends to be the route that closes faster.

I'll get a proposed redline and an evidence checklist from legal tomorrow. If you want a short call, we can do 30 minutes tomorrow afternoon.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-13T10:05:00-08:00
Subject: RE: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Ben / Marissa —

Quick legal read and proposed compromise below. I also attached a redlined order form (redline_order_form_v3.docx) and an evidence custody template we can share with the customer. Summary: aim to keep scope limited to third-party third-party IP claims for customer-visible outputs, carve out training-data provenance claims unless Novacorp can provide demonstrable provenance, and provide a clear evidence production timeline.

Proposed points for order form:
- Scope: Redwood will indemnify Customer for third-party claims alleging that the unmodified output of a Redwood-hosted model infringing a third party's intellectual property, to the extent such output was generated using the Customer's inputs and configuration. Exclude claims arising from: (a) Customer's instructions that replicate known third-party content; (b) Customer-provided training data that contains third-party proprietary material; (c) downstream modifications by Customer or its end users.
- Defense control: Redwood will have the right to assume defense, but Customer may assume defense if Redwood fails to timely defend or if mutual conflict arises. Redwood will not settle any claim that admits fault on behalf of Customer without Customer's consent.
- Notice & cooperation: Customer must provide prompt written notice of claim and provide reasonable cooperation. Redwood to provide an initial evidence package within 30 days of notice (logs, prompt history, model id + version, request timestamps) and a fuller evidence bundle within 60 days (see evidence_custody_template.xlsx for checklist).
- Caps & remedies: Liability for indemnity claims to be subject to an aggregate cap equal to the greater of (i) fees paid by Customer under the applicable order form in the prior 12 months, or (ii) USD 1,000,000. Exclude exemplary/punitive damages.
- Training-data carveout: Recommend explicit carveout for claims based solely on Customer-provided training data unless Customer can produce provenance demonstrating proprietary ownership. That protects Redwood's ability to host customer fine-tuning workloads.

Operational notes (evidence custody):
- We can stage a shared S3 bucket for flagged sessions (read-only for Novacorp/legal) with audit logs.
- Redaction: we will redact third-party PII on a good-faith basis and document any redactions.
- Chain of custody: maintain md5 checksums and a signed manifest for any exported artifacts.

If procurement pushes back on the training-data carveout, recommended fallback: cap indemnity exposure for training-data-origin claims at $250k and require Customer to bear settlement costs absent demonstrable provenance.

I'll be available for a call with Liam/procurement this afternoon at 3pm PT if you want to align before sending.

-Kim
Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
Attachments: redline_order_form_v3.docx; evidence_custody_template.xlsx


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Liam O'Connor <liam@novacorp.com>
Cc: procurement@novacorp.com, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-13T13:25:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Liam —

Thanks for sending the questionnaire. Kimberley and I discussed and attached a proposed redline that we think is reasonable and should be acceptable to procurement. High-level summary of the approach we are proposing:

- Narrow indemnity to third-party IP claims tied to unmodified outputs from our hosted models when generated using Customer inputs/config.
- Redwood to have primary defense control with clear guardrails; Customer may assume defense in limited circumstances.
- Evidence production timeline: initial package within 30 days (logs, prompt history, model/version/timestamps), full bundle within 60 days. We will stage evidence to a shared S3 bucket: https://s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/redwood-evidence/novacorp-session-12345 (read-only for Novacorp legal).
- Training-data claims are carved out unless Novacorp can produce provenance showing proprietary ownership; fallback compromise proposed in the redline.
- Cap: greater of prior 12 months' fees or $1M.

Next steps: we uploaded the redline (redline_order_form_v3.docx) and the evidence checklist (evidence_custody_template.xlsx). If that looks ok to you, I can send the redline for signature (DocuSign: https://docusign.com/link/abc123) and start the operational onboarding for evidence staging.

Would you like to quick-call to run through the carveouts now or would procurement prefer to review with counsel first?

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Attachments: redline_order_form_v3.docx; evidence_custody_template.xlsx


From: Liam O'Connor <liam@novacorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@novacorp.com
Date: 2026-11-14T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Ben / Kim —

Thanks — I shared the proposed language with procurement and our legal counsel. A few quick items:

- Procurement is generally comfortable with the defense-control compromise.
- Counsel is asking for one explicit carveout exception: claims that arise solely from Customer-provided training data (they want that to be excluded from Redwood indemnity). Your proposed fallback of a limited cap for training-data-origin claims looks reasonable, but counsel is asking whether we can get a stronger operational guarantee (e.g., an escrow contact for evidence retention and a named custodian).
- They also want confirmation that Redwood's audit logs will include prompt inputs (not just session ids) — they need that to validate provenance in the event of a dispute.

If you can send the redline with the escrow/custodian contact and confirm the audit-log fields (we need prompt inputs, timestamps, model id/version), I think counsel can move quickly. Procurement is targeting signature before month-end.

—Liam

Date: 2026-11-14T10:02:00-08:00
Subject: Fwd: Re: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Kim / Marissa —

Procurement asked for stronger operational guarantees and an escrow/custodian contact for evidence retention. Also want explicit confirmation that audit logs will include prompt inputs. Can we update the redline to: (a) add a named escrow contact and retention SLA; (b) confirm audit logs include prompt inputs, timestamps, model id/version and are retained for 12 months by default; and (c) rework the training-data carveout to call out that claims solely based on Customer-provided training data are excluded unless provenance is shown, otherwise fallback to the limited cap.

Appreciate a quick take — procurement is pushing for month-end signature.

Thanks,
Ben

Agree — we should: (1) add an escrow/custodian contact and 12-month retention SLA in the order form; (2) confirm in the evidence template that audit logs include prompt inputs, request timestamps, model id and version; and (3) broaden the training data carveout language to be explicit and set the fallback cap at $250k for training-data-origin-only claims.

I'll get a proposed redline and an evidence checklist from legal tomorrow. If you want a short call, we can do 30 minutes tomorrow afternoon.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-13T10:05:00-08:00
Subject: RE: Indemnity carveouts & evidence custody — Novacorp order form

Ben / Marissa —

Quick legal read and proposed compromise below. I also attached a redlined order form (redline_order_form_v3.docx) and an evidence custody template we can share with the customer. Summary: aim to keep scope limited to third-party third-party IP claims for customer-visible outputs, carve out training-data provenance claims unless Novacorp can provide demonstrable provenance, and provide a clear evidence production timeline.

Proposed points for order form:
- Scope: Redwood will indemnify Customer for third-party claims alleging that the unmodified output of a Redwood-hosted model infringing a third party's intellectual property, to the extent such output was generated using the Customer's inputs and configuration. Exclude claims arising from: (a) Customer's instructions that replicate known third-party content; (b) Customer-provided training data that contains third-party proprietary material; (c) downstream modifications by Customer or its end users.
- Defense control: Redwood will have the right to assume defense, but Customer may assume defense if Redwood fails to timely defend or if mutual conflict arises. Redwood will not settle any claim that admits fault on behalf of Customer without Customer's consent.
- Notice & cooperation: Customer must provide prompt written notice of claim and provide reasonable cooperation. Redwood to provide an initial evidence package within 30 days of notice (logs, prompt history, model id + version, request timestamps) and a fuller evidence bundle within 60 days (see evidence_custody_template.xlsx for checklist).
- Caps & remedies: Liability for indemnity claims to be subject to an aggregate cap equal to the greater of (i) fees paid by Customer under the applicable order form in the prior 12 months, or (ii) USD 1,000,000. Exclude exemplary/punitive damages.
- Training-data carveout: Recommend explicit carveout for claims based solely on Customer-provided training data unless Customer can produce provenance demonstrating proprietary ownership. That protects Redwood's ability to host customer fine-tuning workloads.

Operational notes (evidence custody):
- We can stage a shared S3 bucket for flagged sessions (read-only for Novacorp/legal) with audit logs.
- Redaction: we will redact third-party PII on a good-faith basis and document any redactions.
- Chain of custody: maintain md5 checksums and a signed manifest for any exported artifacts.

If procurement pushes back on the training-data carveout, recommended fallback: cap indemnity exposure for training-data-origin claims at $250k and require Customer to bear settlement costs absent demonstrable provenance.

I'll be available for a call with Liam/procurement this afternoon at 3pm PT if you want to align before sending.

-Kim
Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
Attachments: redline_order_form_v3.docx; evidence_custody_template.xlsx

- Narrow indemnity to third-party IP claims tied to unmodified outputs from our hosted models when generated using Customer inputs/config.
- Redwood to have primary defense control with clear guardrails; Customer may assume defense in limited circumstances.
- Evidence production timeline: initial package within 30 days (logs, prompt history, model/version/timestamps), full bundle within 60 days. We will stage evidence to a shared S3 bucket: https://s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/redwood-evidence/novacorp-session-12345 (read-only for Novacorp legal).
- Training-data claims are carved out unless Novacorp can produce provenance showing proprietary ownership; fallback compromise proposed in the redline.
- Cap: greater of prior 12 months' fees or $1M.

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>

Subject: Hosted API pricing ask + commitment (DeltaEdge)

Laura —

Thanks again for the time earlier. Per our conversation, DeltaEdge is prepared to commit to a 12-month, US-only Hosted API contract with an initial monthly commit of 120M generation tokens (expected ramp to 200M/mo by month 6) in exchange for predictable unit pricing. They are requesting a 25% unit-rate reduction vs our standard published hosted rates for that commit window.

Key points:
- Account: DeltaEdge (series B, vertical: supply-chain analytics)
- Initial commit: 120M generation tokens / month; expected to scale to ~200M/mo within 6 months
- Contract length: 12 months; renewal optional with review at month 10
- Requested concession: 25% off standard Hosted API per-token rate for committed volume
- Other asks: SOC2 docs, VPC peering for traffic ingress, and a one-page mutual action plan

I attached the updated MMP and our working pricing model for transparency. This ask exceeds the typical deal-desk threshold (>20%), so we need executive approval for an exception. DeltaEdge has indicated timeline pressure — they aim to sign before Thanksgiving.

Why I think we should consider it:
- Net-new strategic account in manufacturing/supply chain with strong usage profile; good reference value in North America
- Predictable committed volume with rapid ramp reduces short-term volatility in hosted capacity planning
- We can structure guardrails: 12-month term, commit true-ups, and an early-renewal cap tied to consumption tiers

Requested action: Exec authorization for up to 25% concession on committed Hosted API pricing for 12 months, with the above guardrails. If approved, I will finalize the SOW and coordinate security and legal prerequisites.

Thanks — happy to jump on a quick call if helpful.

Best,
Ben

Subject: Internal: DeltaEdge exec approval ask (25% requested) — numbers + recommendation

Team — quick internal note and ask for signoff path. DeltaEdge is pushing for a decision by 24 Nov. Below are the economics and my recommendation.

Simple economics (monthly committed band):
- Published hosted unit rate (baseline): $0.0125 per 1k gen tokens
- 25% off -> Effective rate: $0.009375 per 1k gen tokens
- Initial commit: 120,000k tokens -> monthly invoice at effective rate = $1,125.00
- Expected ramp to 200,000k tokens -> monthly invoice = $1,875.00
- Annualized committed revenue (year 1, conservative ramp schedule): ~$18k – this is modest ARR but high usage velocity and visibility into production workload

Upside / mitigations:
- Reference potential with 2 pilot customers in their enterprise cohort; good marketing + cross-sell signal
- Guardrails: 12-month term, commit true-up, auto-suspend thresholds beyond gross overage without executive clearance
- Option: include a limited add-on for a cost-recovery integration fee for VPC peering and expedited SOC2 evidence prep

Risk: unit economics on token-heavy workloads are material; >20% is outside typical delegated authority. However, given the strategic value and the short fixed term, I recommend we approve a 25% concession for 12 months conditioned on legal-approved SOW and procurement milestones.

Requested: Marcus — can you provide executive signoff or a path to escalate to the C-suite? Mina — please confirm finance comfort with the guardrails and whether we need an alternate billing floor.

Thanks,
Ben

title: Request: soft-preemption carveout & tail-latency guarantees for reserved nodes
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Samira Patel <samira@helixai.com>, Todd Lee <todd.ops@aurora.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: soft-preemption carveout & tail-latency guarantees for reserved nodes

Hi Samira / Todd —

Thanks again for the deep dive yesterday. Per HelixAI's high-frequency inference workloads, you asked us to document a proposal to reduce observed end-to-end 95-99.9th tail latency variance when using Dedicated reserved GPUs, specifically around preemption behavior and nightly batch windows.

We are proposing a \"soft-preemption carveout\" for up to 10% of a customer's reserved node pool during their critical windows (your peak inference hours). Under the carveout:
- Reserved nodes in the carveout will not be eligible for preemptible bursting or eviction for a configurable window (default 4 hours/day).
- Queueing policy will prioritize pinned requests from this carveout with a capped concurrency multiplier to avoid starvation.
- If capacity pressure forces temporary routing, requests will fail-over to a compatible quantized model variant with degraded SLO but automatic alerting to Helix ops.

We think this approach balances platform flexibility and your requirement for predictable tail behavior. Attached is a draft SLA clause (helixai_sla_draft_v3.pdf) and a small sample of traces illustrating the tail events in question (tail_latency_sample_traces.zip).

Can you confirm the typical daily window you want pinned? Also want to check whether Helix prefers hard guarantees (SLA credit formula) or a contractual playbook (runbook + credits on major violations).

Next steps I propose: internal engineering alignment at Redwood (Ben + David) and a short runbook draft for the carveout. If Helix signs off, we’ll schedule a 2-week pilot on a small slice of your reserved pool.

Thanks,
Aisha
---
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha_rahman@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0132

Attachments: helixai_sla_draft_v3.pdf (PDF), tail_latency_sample_traces.zip (ZIP)

From: Samira Patel <samira@helixai.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Todd Lee <todd.ops@aurora.ai>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Request: soft-preemption carveout & tail-latency guarantees for reserved nodes

Aisha —

Thanks for the clear write-up and the draft SLA. Helix is definitely aligned on the need for a carveout window. For our production traffic the busiest window is US-PST 14:00-18:00 (we run spike-prone inference during east-coast business hours). A few clarifications: 

1) We need the carveout to cover at least 25% of our reserved pool during that window (not 10%). Can Redwood support a higher carveout percentage?
2) The fail-over to a quantized model variant is acceptable only if the degradation is <12% in quality (we will provide the eval set).
3) On credits: we'd prefer an SLA credit that has a clear formula (minutes below threshold => credits), but we'll consider a hybrid playbook if Redwood can guarantee a mitigation timeline (e.g., within 30 minutes of an incident, automated fallback and a manual remediation path).

Todd and I are available for a pilot kickoff next week. Please let us know what you need from our side (telemetry, eval prompts, or account access).

Best,
Samira
---
Samira Patel
Head of Platform Reliability, HelixAI
samira@helixai.com


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
Cc: David Kim <david_kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 08:27:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Request: soft-preemption carveout & tail-latency guarantees for reserved nodes

Aisha / Lauren —

Read Samira's ask. Operational notes from infra:
- We can support a 25% carveout, but it changes engineering constraints: carveout nodes will be excluded from multi-tenant preemption and burst pools. That means we need a dedicated headroom budget; effectively ~1.15x token-hour commitment for capacity planning (to account for warmups/heterogeneous load).
- Queueing change: we'll implement a weighted priority queue for pinned traffic with a concurrency cap. Key risk: if pinned traffic spikes beyond provisioned concurrency, requests will queue and potentially hit client-side timeouts. We should set a recommended client retry policy and a server-side circuit breaker (configurable).
- For the quality degradation requirement on quantized fallback, we need Helix evals to validate. If the quantized variant fails the <12% threshold, we will negotiate a different fallback (e.g., offload to a small burst of on-demand instances for short periods).

Action items:
1) Aisha: ask Helix to provide the eval set + expected acceptable throughput during the window.
2) Lauren: draft a simple runbook + suggested SLA credit formula (minutes below 95th/99th thresholds => credit percent).
3) David: confirm whether our monitoring can detect a soft-preemption event and auto-trigger fallback within your 30 min target. If not, propose automation work estimate.

Ben
---
Ben Carter
Director, Dedicated Products | Redwood Inference

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Samira Patel <samira@helixai.com>, Todd Lee <todd.ops@aurora.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, David Kim <david_kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 16:20:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Request: soft-preemption carveout & tail-latency guarantees for reserved nodes

Samira / Todd —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the clear window. Summary and proposed next steps:

Summary of agreement points:
- Redwood will provision a 25% soft-preemption carveout for Helix's reserved pool during PST 14:00-18:00.
- Queueing: pinned traffic uses weighted priority with a per-request concurrency cap; recommended client retry policy: exponential backoff up to 3 retries with 5s initial delay.
- Fallback: quantized-variant allowed if its evaluated quality delta <= 12%; otherwise Redwood will run short-lived on-demand instances as fallback (we'll size these for expected spike percent provided by Helix).
- Credits: Lauren will propose a simple credit formula; initial proposal is 5% credit for every 30 minutes below the agreed 99th percentile latency target, capped at 50% per month.

Requested from Helix to start pilot:
- Eval set and target quality thresholds for the quantized model (upload to the Drive link).
- Expected steady-state throughput and max spike % during the 14:00-18:00 window.
- Confirmation that Helix accepts the proposed client retry recommendation.

I'll schedule a 30-minute kickoff with engineering next Tue. Attaching the SLA draft with tracked changes and sample traces we used to size the fallback.

If this looks correct, Samira/Todd please reply with \"ACK\" and attach the eval set or a drive link.

Thanks,
Aisha
---
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0132

title: Dedicated capacity qualification — OpsAI: reserved GPUs, autoscale & isolation
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sofia Ramirez <sofia@opsai.com>, opsai-sales@opsai.com
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 15:10:00 -0800
Subject: Recap and next steps — Dedicated capacity qualification

Hi Sofia — thanks again for the conversation today. Wanted to capture what we heard and the proposed next steps so everyone is aligned. Short recap below.

Pain points (what you told us)
- Production inference variability: spikes cause timeouts on your inference gateway and downstream customer-facing endpoints.
- Security/isolation mandate: model weights and request vectors must be isolated from multi-tenant co-tenants (audit requirement).
- Predictability: finance needs a predictable cost profile for forecasting; OPS needs throughput guarantees for peak campaigns.

Current stack
- Models served via an in-house Triton wrapper on k8s; model sizes range 7B–70B, primarily PyTorch backends.
- Average concurrent requests: baseline ~350 TPS, peaks to ~950 TPS during campaigns.
- Using 16-bit quantization for most models; occasional FP16 for latency-sensitive models.

Success criteria for Dedicated fit
- Predictable p95 latency <= 120ms for typical chat generation path.
- Sustained baseline throughput of ~500 TPS with bursts to ~1k TPS for up to 10–15 minutes.
- Logical isolation (reserved GPU pools) and auditability (KMS logs, SOC-2 evidence).
- Cost predictability: a committed monthly spend with clear burst pricing.

Proposed immediate next steps (for this week)
1) OpsAI to share a 1‑hour production trace or a small traffic replay (anonymized) — file: OpsAI_prod_profile_sample.json.
2) Redwood to run a quick sizing pass and propose a baseline reserve (typical starting point: 8–12 reserved GPUs depending on model mix) — internal note: Laura will lead sizing.
3) Schedule a benchmarking call (30–45m) with your infra + our Solutions Eng to scope autoscale/burst policies — propose Wed 11/17 or Thu 11/18.
4) Share a draft pricing / SOW for committed capacity — attached Redwood_Dedicated_pricing_v2.pdf and SOW_Draft_OpsAI.docx (stub).

If this captures things accurately, please reply with the trace file and preferred time for the benchmarking call. Happy to sync on any other requirements (data residency, additional compliance).

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0198

Attachments: OpsAI_prod_profile_sample.json (stub), Redwood_Dedicated_pricing_v2.pdf (stub)

From: Sofia Ramirez <sofia@opsai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: opsai-sales@opsai.com
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 18:03:00 -05:00
Subject: Re: Recap and next steps — Dedicated capacity qualification

Thanks Ben — this is great and aligns with our notes. A few clarifications and add-ons from my side: 

- Trace file: we'll provide a 1-hour replay (anonymized) by EOD Wednesday; it will include both chat and batch rerank traffic.
- Model mix detail: 70% 7B–13B models for conversational features, 30% 34B+ for summarization/rerank.
- Residency: primary requirement is US (we may expand to EU later).
- SLA ask: we'd like a documented approach to fallbacks — if reserved pool is exhausted, what's the transparent rollback/fallback to hosted or degraded mode?

We can do benchmarking call Thu 11/18 10:00am PT. Laura / your SE are welcome to invite our infra lead if helpful (cc'ed opsai-sales).

Sofia Ramirez
Head of ML Ops, OpsAI
sofia@opsai.com | +1 617-555-0123

On another note — we will need SOC 2 evidence and basic pen-test reports for security review. I assume Redwood has a standard pack you can share.

---


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Nov 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Internal: quick sizing take and benchmarking plan

Ben / Rishi — quick internal note before the benchmarking call:

Initial sizing assumptions (rough, will refine with replay)
- Baseline target 500 TPS sustained: estimate 6 x H100-equivalent slots (multi-model packing + caching).
- Burst target 1k TPS for 10–15m: recommend baseline reserve + 2–3 burst nodes (hot standby) with autoscale policy that allows short-lived capacity spikes without overcommit.
- Isolation: leased-reserved pools per customer (no co-tenancy with external tenants) — include KMS key per customer and audit logs.

Benchmark plan for Thu 11/18
- Bring an H100 test rig and run model replay with same tokenization and batch distributions.
- Measure p50/p95 latency, throughput knee, and memory/kv-cache behavior.
- Validate fallback plan: simulate reserved pool saturation and observe automatic routing to burst pool or fallback variants.

Deliverables after call: sizing deck (1-pager), configuration recommendations (batch size, KV caching hints), and updated pricing delta for burst minutes.

I'll draft the sizing deck right after the call and attach to the HubSpot deal.

- Laura
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

title: Operationalizing streaming funcalls + ranking — recap & next steps
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sam Levin <sam.levin@monarchhealth.com>, Priya Rao <priya.rao@monarchhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 15:05:00 -0800
Subject: Operationalizing streaming funcalls + ranking — recap & next steps

Hi Sam / Priya — thanks again for taking the time today. Short recap from our call and proposed next steps so we’re all aligned.

Key pain you described
- Clinical triage team needs sub-second follow-up suggestions based on multi-turn intake (streaming) but current endpoint stalls on long context.
- Existing pipeline has a reranking stage (BM25 -> model rerank) that’s adding 200–400ms per request and is brittle for new document types (discharge notes).
- Security needs: logs of requests retained 90 days, and model outputs must be redactable/traceable for audits.

Goals we agreed on
- Prototype a streaming + funcall flow that produces partial responses while calling into a structured-output function to populate triage fields.
- Replace the brittle rerank step with an embeddings + light model reranker (embedding similarity candidate recall + small reranker model) to reduce latency and improve stability.
- Deliver a short pilot activity: 2-week sandbox, integration checklist, and a low-risk canary to validate latency & correctness.

Technical constraints you mentioned
- Max end-to-end P95 latency target: 350ms for simple triage flows.
- Maximum 2k token working set in the client (we will suggest KV caching for static patient snippets).
- SOC2 + SCIM provisioning required before sandbox access.

Immediate next steps (owners & timing)
1) Redwood: share a short integration checklist + sample prompt set (attachment) — by Thu 11/12 — owner: Ava.
2) Monarch: provide a representative triage conversation log (anonymized) + a list of must-have structured fields — by Mon 11/16 — owner: Priya.
3) Tech sync (30m) to walk the SDK changes required for streaming and funcalls: Thu 11/19 (propose 10am PT) — owners: Ben + Sam.
4) Security kickoff for SOC2 evidence & SCIM: week of 11/23 — owner: Sergio.

Follow-ups we’ll include in the proposal
- Option A: streaming + funcall path using Redwood streaming SDK + structured output schema. Estimated infra delta: low (we’ll reuse your pool of small vector store nodes).
- Option B: batching + cached KV approach for longer contexts if streaming cannot meet 350ms in initial pilot.

Attachments: integration-proposal.pdf (proposal outline), sample-prompt-set.csv (example prompts + reranker seed queries).

If that reads correctly, I’ll send a formal sandbox invite and share the Drive folder with the proposal.

Thanks — Ava

--
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0192
https://redwood.inference |  


From: Sam Levin <sam.levin@monarchhealth.com>

Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 17:12:00 -0800

Thanks Ava — that summary is spot on. A couple clarifications from our side: 

- The 350ms P95 is for the 3–5 field triage flow (the longer flows can be 600–700ms while we still feel comfortable).
- The reranker is currently a heuristic layer that drops many relevant docs; replacing with lightweight reranker + embedding candidates sounds promising.
- We can send an anonymized week of triage logs (approx 1k sessions) but need to scrub PHI — Priya will own.

Agree on the proposed next steps. Confirming Thu 11/19 10am PT works for Ben + your team. Please include a short demo of the streaming SDK behavior (partial tokens + structured funcall JSON) in the sync.

Monarch Health

title: BrightLayer SaaS consumption mapping & fulfillment questions
preview:
From: Sophie Lane <sophie.lane@brightlayer.ai>
To: chloe.martin@redwood.ai
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: AWS marketplace metering questions for BrightLayer

Hi Chloe,

Thanks again for the demo last week — our engineering team is excited about Redwood's batching and entitlement hooks. We're starting to scope the AWS Marketplace integration and have a couple of concrete questions before we formalize the mutual action plan: 

1) Entitlement identifier: can Redwood accept a customer-provided entitlement_id (UUID) from AWS fulfillment webhook and map it directly to our tenant id, or do we need to mint a Redwood-side entitlement as well?

2) Usage dimensions: we have two consumption metrics (API calls and compute-seconds). Does the Redwood metering adapter support multi-dimension usage reporting to AWS, or should we consolidate to a single normalized unit for Marketplace usage?

3) Billing alignment: if a customer subscribes through Marketplace but uses Dedicated Redwood capacity, what's the recommended reconciliation flow for credits and invoice backfill?

We're also collecting the security and billing contact for procurement. Looping in procurement@brightlayer.ai as well.

Would you be available for a short technical run-through on Thursday morning PST?

Best,
Sophie Lane
Head of Platform Partnerships
BrightLayer, Inc.

---
Attachments: none


From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
To: Sophie Lane <sophie.lane@brightlayer.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 16:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: AWS marketplace metering questions for BrightLayer

Hi Sophie — great to hear, and thanks for the clear list. Thursday AM PST works for me; I'm penciling in 9:30am PT. Invite to follow.

Quick answers up front so your engineers have something to chew on before the call: 

- Entitlement mapping: Redwood can ingest the AWS fulfillment entitlement_id and map it to your tenant id. We recommend a one-to-one mapping and storing the AWS entitlement as an external key in our entitlement object (no need to mint a separate customer-visible id). That approach avoids duplicate lookups when fulfilling usage.

- Multi-dimension reporting: our metering adapter supports multiple dimensions. We expose usage dimensions in the fulfillment webhook mapping and can send API_CALLS and COMPUTE_SECONDS as separate usage dimensions to AWS, but note that some Marketplace billing SKUs want a single quantity — we can provide an aggregation transform if you prefer a normalized unit.

- Billing + Dedicated capacity: common pattern is to reconcile monthly: Marketplace issues the customer invoice to AWS, you pass the Marketplace invoice reference into Redwood entitlement metadata so our billing service can attribute consumption, and then for Dedicated we apply credits/offsets via a manual settlement file or via the Marketplace refund path depending on contract. We will walk through the options on the call.

I attached a lightweight integration checklist to this thread to get us prepared.

Chloe
Director, Partnerships
Redwood Inference

---

1) Entitlement mapping (Ben) — Redwood will treat AWS entitlement_uuid as external_entitlement.id and write it into the tenant metadata. Ben will own a sample webhook payload and test harness by 2026-11-18.

2) Usage dimensions (Vivek) — we can report two dimensions (API_CALLS, COMPUTE_SECONDS). Vivek will validate dimension names against AWS SKU constraints and provide mapping guidance by 2026-11-16. If BrightLayer prefers a normalized unit for Marketplace SKU, we will provide an aggregator function.

Hi Sophie — great to hear, and thanks for the clear list. Thursday AM PST works for me; I'm penciling in 9:30am PT. Invite to follow.

Quick answers up front so your engineers have something to chew on before the call: 

- Entitlement mapping: Redwood can ingest the AWS fulfillment entitlement_id and map it to your tenant id. We recommend a one-to-one mapping and storing the AWS entitlement as an external key in our entitlement object (no need to mint a separate customer-visible id). That approach avoids duplicate lookups when fulfilling usage.

- Multi-dimension reporting: our metering adapter supports multiple dimensions. We expose usage dimensions in the fulfillment webhook mapping and can send API_CALLS and COMPUTE_SECONDS as separate usage dimensions to AWS, but note that some Marketplace billing SKUs want a single quantity — we can provide an aggregation transform if you prefer a normalized unit.

- Billing + Dedicated capacity: common pattern is to reconcile monthly: Marketplace issues the customer invoice to AWS, you pass the Marketplace invoice reference into Redwood entitlement metadata so our billing service can attribute consumption, and then for Dedicated we apply credits/offsets via a manual settlement file or via the Marketplace refund path depending on contract. We will walk through the options on the call.

I attached a lightweight integration checklist to this thread to get us prepared.

Chloe
Director, Partnerships
Redwood Inference
